subject: reading
title: Grade 3 Reading — EOG Practice Test 6
instructions: Read each selection carefully. Then answer the questions. Choose the one best answer for each question unless you are told to select more than one.

passages.title: The Kindness Rocks
passages.text:
Part 1

Maya found the smooth gray rock half buried in the sand at the edge of the lake. Someone had painted a tiny yellow sun on it, and under the sun were two words: BE BRAVE. Maya turned the rock over and over in her hands. She did not know who had left it, but it made her smile on a morning when she had felt anything but brave.

That afternoon, Maya carried the rock home in her pocket. Her family had just moved to a new town, and tomorrow she would start at a new school where she knew no one. All week her stomach had felt like a jar full of buzzing bees. But every time she touched the little painted sun, the buzzing grew a bit quieter.

The next morning, Maya stood at the door of room 12. Her heart pounded. Then she slipped her hand into her pocket and felt the smooth rock there. BE BRAVE, it reminded her. She took a deep breath and walked inside.

Part 2

After school, Maya had an idea. If one painted rock could help her feel brave, maybe she could help someone else feel brave too. She gathered a basket of smooth stones from the yard, found her mother's paints, and got to work.

For a week, Maya painted. She made rocks with rainbows, stars, hearts, and tiny suns. On each one she wrote a few kind words: YOU MATTER. KEEP GOING. YOU ARE NOT ALONE. Then she began leaving them in quiet places around town—on a park bench, beside the library steps, near the door of room 12.

Maya never signed her name, and she never told anyone it was her. But on the playground she noticed a boy holding a rock painted with a star, grinning as if he had found treasure. A girl tucked a heart rock carefully into her backpack. Maya hugged her own rock and felt warm all the way through. She had learned that the kindness someone had given her was a gift she could pass along, one painted stone at a time.

passages.title: How Roadrunner Won the Race
passages.text:
Part 1

Long ago, when the desert was young and the animals could still boast and brag, Rabbit believed he was the fastest creature in all the land. "No one can beat me," he announced from the top of a tall red rock. "My legs are like springs, and my feet barely touch the ground!"

The other animals were tired of Rabbit's bragging. Quiet little Roadrunner, who spent her days darting between the cactus plants, stepped forward. "I will race you," she said softly. Rabbit laughed so hard he nearly fell off his rock. "You? Race me? This will be the easiest win of my life!"

The animals marked a path from the dry riverbed to the old twisted juniper tree and back. "Tomorrow at sunrise," said wise old Tortoise, who would be the judge.

Part 2

At sunrise the next morning, every animal in the desert gathered to watch. Tortoise raised his foot and the race began. Rabbit shot ahead in a cloud of dust, so far in front that he could no longer even see Roadrunner behind him.

"This is too easy," Rabbit chuckled. "I will rest in the shade and still win." He curled up beneath a cool mesquite tree and closed his eyes, certain he had plenty of time. But Roadrunner did not run in great leaps that wore her out. Instead, she ran with a steady patter, her feet whispering across the sand, never once stopping to rest.

While Rabbit dozed, Roadrunner passed him quietly. By the time Rabbit yawned and stretched and remembered the race, Roadrunner was already crossing the finish line. The animals cheered until the canyon rang. From that day on, Rabbit did far less bragging, and Roadrunner became known across the desert not as the loudest, but as the one who kept going. And to this very day, you can still see Roadrunner racing across the sand, steady and quick, with no need to boast at all.

passages.title: From Egg to Frog
passages.text:
Part 1

A frog does not begin its life looking like a frog at all. It begins as a tiny egg floating in a pond. A mother frog may lay hundreds of eggs at once, joined together in a soft, jellylike cluster. The clear jelly protects the eggs and keeps them from drying out while the young frogs grow inside.

After about a week, the eggs hatch. But the creatures that wriggle out are not frogs—they are tadpoles. A tadpole looks more like a tiny fish. It has a long tail for swimming and gills on the sides of its body that let it breathe underwater. At this stage, a tadpole eats plants that grow in the pond.

Part 2

Over the next several weeks, the tadpole begins to change in an amazing way. First, two tiny back legs push out near its tail. A little later, two front legs appear. Slowly, the tadpole's tail grows shorter and shorter as its body uses up the tail for energy.

Inside its body, even bigger changes are happening. The tadpole grows lungs to replace its gills, so that it can breathe air instead of water. Now the young frog can leave the pond and hop onto land for the first time. This whole change from one body shape into a very different one is called metamorphosis.

The grown frog will spend much of its life on land, catching insects with its long, sticky tongue. But when it is time to lay eggs of its own, the frog will return to the water where its life began. And there, in the quiet pond, the amazing journey from egg to frog will start all over again.

passages.title: Bessie Coleman: Reaching for the Sky
passages.text:
Part 1

Bessie Coleman was born in Texas in 1892, the tenth of thirteen children. Her family worked hard picking cotton, and there was little time or money for big dreams. But Bessie loved to read and learn, and she was determined to make something of her life. When she grew up, she moved to the city of Chicago, where she worked and listened to the stories of pilots returning from far away.

Bessie made up her mind: she would learn to fly an airplane. In those days, however, flight schools in the United States would not accept her because she was a woman and because she was Black. Most people would have given up. Bessie did something bold instead.

Part 2

Bessie learned that flight schools in France would teach her, so she studied the French language at night after work. Then she sailed all the way across the ocean to France to chase her dream. There, in 1921, Bessie Coleman earned her pilot's license. She became the first Black woman in the world to do so.

When Bessie returned home, crowds gathered to watch her fly. She performed in air shows, looping and rolling her plane high above the cheering crowds. People called her "Brave Bessie," and she became famous across the country.

But Bessie wanted to do more than thrill a crowd. She dreamed of opening a flying school so that other young Black students could learn to fly, just as she had longed to. She gave speeches and refused to perform anywhere that would not allow Black and white people to watch together. Bessie Coleman did not live to open her school, but her courage opened the skies for those who came after her, proving that a determined dream is stronger than any wall in its way.

passages.title: The Little Brook
passages.text:
Part 1

High on the hill where the cold springs start,
A little brook begins its part.
It tumbles and trickles down mossy stone,
Singing a song all its very own.

It does not hurry, it does not rest,
It simply does what it does best:
It slips past the roots of the old oak tree,
And carries a leaf like a boat to sea.

Part 2

The brook grows wider as it goes,
Gathering rain and melting snows.
The deer bend low to take a drink,
The minnows dart along its brink.

It turns the wheel of the mill in town,
It waters the fields of golden brown.
Then on it rolls, both night and day,
Until it meets a river's way.

So here is a lesson the small brook knows:
Keep moving gently wherever it goes.
A little water, steady and true,
Can shape a valley before it is through.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, where does Maya find the painted rock?
options: On a park bench
Near the door of room 12
Half buried in the sand at the edge of the lake
Beside the library steps
questions.explanation: Part 1 says Maya found the smooth gray rock half buried in the sand at the edge of the lake.

questions.questionText: What does the phrase "her stomach had felt like a jar full of buzzing bees" help the reader understand in Part 1?
options: Maya kept bees as pets.
Maya was hungry for breakfast.
Maya was excited about the lake.
Maya was feeling very nervous.
questions.explanation: Comparing her stomach to a jar of buzzing bees shows that Maya felt nervous and uneasy about her new school.

questions.questionText: Why was Maya nervous about the next morning?
options: She was going to give a speech.
She was starting at a new school where she knew no one.
She had to swim across the lake.
She had lost her painted rock.
questions.explanation: Part 1 explains that Maya's family had just moved and she would start at a new school the next day where she knew no one.

questions.questionText: What does Maya do right after she has the idea to help someone else feel brave?
options: She walks into room 12.
She signs her name on a rock.
She gathers smooth stones and paints them.
She tells her mother her secret.
questions.explanation: In Part 2, after getting the idea, Maya gathers a basket of smooth stones, finds her mother's paints, and gets to work.

questions.questionText: Use both parts of this text to answer this question. What is the main message of the story?
options: It is best to keep your good deeds a secret forever.
Kindness you receive can be passed along to help others.
Moving to a new town is always frightening.
Painting rocks is a good way to earn money.
questions.explanation: Maya receives a kind painted rock that helps her, and then she passes that kindness along by making rocks for others, showing that kindness can be passed along.

questions.questionText: Which word best describes Maya in the story?
options: Thoughtful
Lazy
Careless
Greedy
questions.explanation: Maya thinks about how the rock helped her and chooses to make rocks to help others, which shows she is thoughtful.

questions.questionText: Which detail from the text best shows that Maya's painted rocks made other people happy?
options: "Her family had just moved to a new town."
"...a boy holding a rock painted with a star, grinning as if he had found treasure."
"All week her stomach had felt like a jar full of buzzing bees."
"She did not know who had left it."
questions.explanation: The boy grinning as if he had found treasure while holding one of the rocks shows that the rocks made others happy.

questions.questionText: Select the two kind messages Maya writes on the rocks she makes in Part 2.
options: YOU MATTER
FOLLOW ME
BE BRAVE
KEEP GOING
questions.explanation: In Part 2, Maya writes messages such as YOU MATTER, KEEP GOING, and YOU ARE NOT ALONE on her rocks. BE BRAVE was on the rock she found, not one she made.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, what does Rabbit believe about himself?
options: That he cannot run very far
That he is too tired to race
That he is the fastest creature in all the land
That he is the wisest animal in the desert
questions.explanation: Part 1 says Rabbit believed he was the fastest creature in all the land and that no one could beat him.

questions.questionText: Why does Rabbit decide to rest under the mesquite tree during the race?
options: Roadrunner asks him to wait for her.
He is so far ahead that he thinks he has plenty of time to win.
He has hurt his foot.
Tortoise tells him to take a break.
questions.explanation: Rabbit is so far ahead that he thinks the race is too easy and that he can rest in the shade and still win.

questions.questionText: What does the phrase "her feet whispering across the sand" help the reader understand about Roadrunner in Part 2?
options: Roadrunner moved quietly and steadily.
Roadrunner was too tired to run.
Roadrunner was talking to the sand.
Roadrunner was afraid of the noise.
questions.explanation: Saying her feet were "whispering across the sand" shows that Roadrunner ran quietly and steadily, without loud, tiring leaps.

questions.questionText: What is the lesson of the story?
options: Bragging and resting can cause you to lose; steady effort wins.
Desert animals should not race one another.
It is best to run as fast as you can at the start.
Rabbits are always faster than roadrunners.
questions.explanation: Rabbit brags and stops to rest and loses, while steady Roadrunner keeps going and wins, teaching that steady effort beats bragging.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "boast" as it is used in this story?
options: To brag about yourself
To run very fast
To fall asleep
To feel afraid
questions.explanation: The story shows Rabbit announcing how fast he is and laughing at Roadrunner, so to "boast" means to brag about yourself.

questions.questionText: Which word best describes Roadrunner in the story?
options: Unkind
Determined
Boastful
Fearful
questions.explanation: Roadrunner quietly accepts the challenge and never stops running until she wins, which shows she is determined.

questions.questionText: How does the author organize the events in Part 2 of the story?
options: By comparing the desert to the forest
By telling what happens in order, from the start of the race to the finish
By explaining why deserts are hot
By giving directions for running a race
questions.explanation: Part 2 tells the events of the race in order: the start, Rabbit racing ahead and resting, Roadrunner passing him, and crossing the finish line.

questions.questionText: Who is chosen to be the judge of the race?
options: Roadrunner
The desert deer
Wise old Tortoise
Rabbit
questions.explanation: Part 1 states that wise old Tortoise would be the judge of the race.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, how does a frog's life begin?
options: As a fish in the ocean
As a fully grown frog on land
As an insect in the grass
As a tiny egg floating in a pond
questions.explanation: Part 1 says a frog begins its life as a tiny egg floating in a pond.

questions.questionText: What does a tadpole use to breathe underwater?
options: Its long tail
Its lungs
Its sticky tongue
Gills on the sides of its body
questions.explanation: Part 1 explains that a tadpole has gills on the sides of its body that let it breathe underwater.

questions.questionText: According to Part 2, which legs appear first as the tadpole changes?
options: The two front legs
All four legs at the same time
The tadpole never grows legs
The two back legs
questions.explanation: Part 2 says first two tiny back legs push out near its tail, and a little later the two front legs appear.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "metamorphosis" as it is used in Part 2?
options: A long, sticky tongue
A change from one body shape into a very different one
A kind of pond plant
A cluster of frog eggs
questions.explanation: The text says the whole change from one body shape into a very different one is called metamorphosis.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas in Part 1 and Part 2 of the text?
options: By telling a made-up story about one frog
By listing reasons frogs are helpful to people
By describing the stages of a frog's life in the order they happen
By comparing frogs with fish that live in the ocean
questions.explanation: Part 1 describes the egg and tadpole stages and Part 2 continues with the changes into a frog, presenting the stages of a frog's life in order.

questions.questionText: What is the connection between the tadpole growing lungs and the young frog being able to leave the pond?
options: Growing lungs lets the frog breathe air, so it can move onto land.
Growing lungs has nothing to do with leaving the pond.
Growing lungs makes the frog's tail grow longer.
Growing lungs causes the frog to lay eggs.
questions.explanation: The text shows cause and effect: the tadpole grows lungs to replace its gills so it can breathe air, which allows the young frog to leave the pond and hop onto land.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of the text?
options: Tadpoles look just like tiny fish.
Ponds are full of jellylike eggs.
Frogs eat insects with sticky tongues.
A frog changes through several stages as it grows from an egg into an adult.
questions.explanation: The whole text explains how a frog grows and changes through stages, from egg to tadpole to adult frog.

questions.questionText: Select the two changes that happen to a tadpole as it becomes a frog.
options: Its body turns into an egg.
Its tail grows shorter.
It grows gills to breathe underwater.
It grows lungs to breathe air.
questions.explanation: Part 2 explains that the tadpole's tail grows shorter and that it grows lungs to breathe air. It loses its gills rather than growing them, and it does not turn back into an egg.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, where was Bessie Coleman born?
options: In Texas
In an air show
In France
In Chicago
questions.explanation: Part 1 states that Bessie Coleman was born in Texas in 1892.

questions.questionText: Why did Bessie travel all the way to France?
options: To pick cotton with her family
To watch other pilots in air shows
To open a flying school for students
To learn to fly, because flight schools in the United States would not accept her
questions.explanation: Flight schools in the United States would not accept Bessie because she was a woman and Black, so she went to France, where a school would teach her.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "determined" as it is used in Part 1?
options: Funny and playful
Worried and afraid
Set on doing something and not giving up
Tired and bored
questions.explanation: The text shows Bessie working hard to reach her goal, so "determined" means set on doing something and not giving up.

questions.questionText: What did Bessie do at night to prepare to go to France?
options: She performed in air shows.
She built her own airplane.
She gave speeches to crowds.
She studied the French language.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says Bessie studied the French language at night after work so she could attend a flight school in France.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas in Part 1 about Bessie's problem with the ideas in Part 2?
options: By listing the cities where Bessie performed
By presenting Bessie's problem and then how she solved it
By comparing two different airplanes
By giving steps for flying a plane in order
questions.explanation: Part 1 presents the problem that U.S. flight schools would not accept Bessie, and Part 2 shows the solution: she learned French and went to France to earn her license.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of the text?
options: Bessie Coleman had twelve brothers and sisters.
Air shows were popular long ago.
France was the only place to learn to fly.
Bessie Coleman overcame unfair barriers to become a pilot and inspire others.
questions.explanation: The text as a whole shows how Bessie pushed past unfair barriers to become a pilot and how she wanted to help others fly, which is its main idea.

questions.questionText: Which statement from the text best supports the idea that Bessie wanted to help other people, not just herself?
options: "She performed in air shows, looping and rolling her plane high above the cheering crowds."
"She dreamed of opening a flying school so that other young Black students could learn to fly."
"There, in 1921, Bessie Coleman earned her pilot's license."
"Bessie Coleman was born in Texas in 1892, the tenth of thirteen children."
questions.explanation: Her dream of opening a flying school so other young Black students could learn to fly shows she wanted to help others, not just herself.

questions.questionText: Which word best describes Bessie Coleman, based on the text?
options: Selfish
Forgetful
Brave
Fearful
questions.explanation: Bessie crossed an ocean, refused to give up, and performed daring flights, and people even called her "Brave Bessie," which shows she was brave.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, where does the little brook begin?
options: In a town near a mill
High on the hill where the cold springs start
At a river's edge
In the fields of golden brown
questions.explanation: Part 1 says the little brook begins high on the hill where the cold springs start.

questions.questionText: What does the poet mean by the line "And carries a leaf like a boat to sea" in Part 1?
options: A floating leaf is carried along the moving water like a little boat.
Sailors row their boats down the brook.
The brook turns into a real boat.
The leaf sinks to the bottom of the brook.
questions.explanation: The line compares a leaf floating on the moving water to a little boat being carried along, helping the reader picture the leaf drifting on the brook.

questions.questionText: How is the poem mainly organized?
options: By following the brook's journey from the hilltop to the river
By describing a single rainy day
By giving steps for building a mill
By comparing two different brooks
questions.explanation: The poem follows the brook from where it starts on the hill, growing wider as it travels, until it meets a river's way.

questions.questionText: What is the main message, or theme, of the poem?
options: Water always moves too quickly to enjoy.
Mills are the most important buildings in a town.
Steady, gentle effort over time can make a big difference.
Brooks are dangerous and should be avoided.
questions.explanation: The final stanza says a little water, steady and true, can shape a valley, teaching that steady, gentle effort over time makes a big difference.

questions.questionText: According to Part 2, what useful work does the brook do in the town?
options: It paves the roads.
It puts out fires.
It turns the wheel of the mill.
It carries boats full of people.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says the brook turns the wheel of the mill in town and waters the fields.

questions.questionText: In Part 1, the poet writes that the brook is "Singing a song all its very own." What does this line help the reader imagine?
options: The bubbling, trickling sound the water makes as it moves
Birds calling from the old oak tree
The silence of a frozen brook
A person standing beside the brook and singing
questions.explanation: Describing the brook as singing a song helps the reader imagine the bubbling, trickling sound the moving water makes.

questions.questionText: Both "How Roadrunner Won the Race" and "The Little Brook" share which idea?
options: Steady, gentle effort over time leads to success.
Deserts and brooks are dangerous places.
A clever trick is the best way to win.
Bragging helps you reach your goals.
questions.explanation: Roadrunner wins by moving steadily without stopping, and the brook shapes a valley by moving steadily over time; both share the idea that steady, gentle effort leads to success.

questions.questionText: Both "Bessie Coleman: Reaching for the Sky" and "From Egg to Frog" describe a kind of change. How are the two changes different?
options: Both changes happen by accident with no effort.
Both changes are choices the subjects make on purpose.
A frog's change is a choice, while Bessie's just happened on its own.
Bessie's change was a choice she worked toward, while a frog's change happens naturally as it grows.
questions.explanation: Bessie chose her goal and worked hard to become a pilot, while a frog's change from egg to adult happens naturally as part of growing up, so the two kinds of change are different.
